title: NSW teens’ tech literacy falling sharply
full_text:
IT literacy among 15 to 16-year-old students in NSW has fallen to record lows, continuing a marked decline over the last decade to drop below the national average.
Last year just over one-third of NSW year 10 students attained a proficient standard, dropping from 44 per cent in 2022 and a sharp decline from a high of 67 per cent in 2008, according to research from the Australian Curriculum, Assessment and Reporting Authority (ACARA).
The results for year 10 students in 2025 place the state in the mid‑range nationally, significantly lower than the ACT, marginally below Victoria and Western Australia, and on par with South Australia.
In contrast, IT literacy for year 6 students was steady, slipping three percentage points since 2017 to just above the 2025 national average.
The ACT recorded the highest results nationally for both year 6 and year 10 students.
NSW’s results mirror a national downward trend in IT literacy for 15 to 16-year-olds despite most students reporting extensive experience using tech. Only half of year 6 students in Australia met national IT benchmarks last year, but the number plummeted to just 37 per cent in year 10 – the lowest figure since testing began in 2005 and a drop of almost 20 per cent since 2022.
Research also shows that AI use is surging, with more than 60 per cent of year 10 students and almost a third of year 6 children using AI to create written content at least once a month. One quarter of year 10s use AI “frequently” at school.
